summary: This Valentine's Day-themed advice column tackles questions about career transitions for math PhDs and various relationship/sex questions. O'Neil advises a math PhD student to consider data science (and plugs her book), while offering a tongue-in-cheek quiz suggesting actuarial math for 'boring' people and finance for 'evil' ones. The sex advice portions emphasize open communication with partners, noting that many couples assume their partner doesn't want something without actually asking. She argues that insisting on any specific sexual act every time 'borders on abusive' and advocates for balance and mutual satisfaction.
key_points: Math PhDs seeking industry careers should consider data science, learn Python, and build a public portfolio of projects
Her satirical career quiz implies actuarial work is boring and finance attracts morally questionable people
Many relationship communication problems stem from assuming what partners want without actually asking them
Sexual relationships require negotiation and balance—no one should insist on the same thing every time
When sharing sexual fantasies, the rule is: share if it increases arousal, keep quiet if it causes jealousy, or adapt it to move from the second category to the first
money_quotes: If she said 'yes' to #1, she might consider actuarial math. If she said 'yes' to #2, she should think finance.

A lot of people complain about their sex life and when I ask them whether they've tried various things, they tell me their partner 'doesn't want that.' But when I ask whether they've asked their partner, explicitly, about that, they admit they haven't.
It's not ok for someone to insist on something, anything, to happen every time, unless both people want it. It borders on abusive, in fact.
Everyone thinks about sex all the time, so by the pigeon hole principle most people think for at least a few minutes a day about any one question.

Share when it will make your partner hornier. Keep it to yourself if it will make your partner angry or jealous or less horny. And third, change it ever so slightly to bring it from the second category to the first.
key_insight: Whether in careers or relationships, people often make assumptions about what's possible or acceptable without actually testing those assumptions through direct communication.
